Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Keep Bellevue Beautiful - West Side Cleanup
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 9 AM

Keep Bellevue Beautiful - West Side Cleanup EReed Tue, 03/10/2026 - 16:17 April 11 2026 9:00 AM - 11:00 AM All Day Event Off Event Registration Free Contact Evan Reed Email ereed@bellevuewa.gov Telephone 425-452-5372 Location City Hall, 450 110th Ave NE Description The City of Bellevue's Keep Bellevue Beautiful program is launching 26 in '26, an effort aimed at completing 26 Signature Street cleanups in 2026. The goal is simple: show up consistently, take care of our streets and support cleaner, more welcoming neighborhoods. Date: Saturday, April 11 Time: 9-11 a.m. Start Location: Bellevue City Hall For this special West Side Cleanup, volunteers will begin at City Hall before choosing their own cleanup route across any of the following neighborhoods: Bridle Trails Northwest Bellevue BelRed Downtown Wilburton Woodridge West Bellevue Factoria Newport Experience is not required, and all cleanup supplies will be provided. Volunteers are welcome to stay for the full two hours or drop in and help for as long as they can. Coffee and water will be available. When you're done, simply leave your filled trash bags and gear at the previously agreed upon location within your cleanup neighborhood. A staff member will retrieve everything at the end of the event. Register: CleanWestBellevue.EventBrite.com Hide from City Calendar Off Canceled Off Location City Hall

Keep Bellevue Beautiful - West Side Cleanup EReed Tue, 03/10/2026 - 16:17 April 11 2026 9:00 AM - 11:00 AM All Day Event Off Event Registration Free Contact Evan Reed Email ereed@bellevuewa.gov Telephone 425-452-5372 Location City Hall, 450 110th Ave NE Description The City of Bellevue's Keep Bellevue Beautiful program is launching 26 in '26, an effort aimed at completing 26 Signature Street cleanups in 2026. The goal is simple: show up consistently, take care of our streets and support cleaner, more welcoming neighborhoods. Date: Saturday, April 11 Time: 9-11 a.m. Start Location: Bellevue City Hall For this special West Side Cleanup, volunteers will begin at City Hall before choosing their own cleanup route across any of the following neighborhoods: Bridle Trails Northwest Bellevue BelRed Downtown Wilburton Woodridge West Bellevue Factoria Newport Experience is not required, and all cleanup supplies will be provided. Volunteers are welcome to stay for the full two hours or drop in and help for as long as they can. Coffee and water will be available. When you're done, simply leave your filled trash bags and gear at the previously agreed upon location within your cleanup neighborhood. A staff member will retrieve everything at the end of the event. Register: CleanWestBellevue.EventBrite.com Hide from City Calendar Off Canceled Off Location City Hall

item.title: Earth Month Event | Restoration at Ellis Pond
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Celebrate Earth Month at our Ellis Pond Work Party Join us at Ellis Pond Park for a Forest-Steward led weeding and clean-up event fo r Earth Month. Removing certain plants will help build even more wildlife habitat in this small neighborhood park teeming with birds and other woodl and creatures. We will remove weeds like English ivy, Himalayan blackberry, and English holly to help protect native plants and forest health. Come join friends and neighbors in this fun Saturday event. No experie nce necessary; gloves and tools are provided. Participation is limited, so please register. Please note: we require participants aged 14 and under to volunteer with a parent or guardian at a ratio of 1 adult to 3 ki ds. When: Saturday, April 11th, 9:30am-11:30pm Where: Ellis Pond Park \(meet at the bench next to the boardwalk on 90th Ave SE\) What to bring: Dress for the weather in clothes you don't mind getting dirty \(think: lon g pants, boots or sneakers\). Bring a water bottle. Small snacks, gloves, and tools will be provided. And a snack or dish to share. Registration : volunteersignup.org/Y8XQX

item.title: Member Family Day: Animal Intelligence
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

SAM members, don't have a room with a view booked this spring break? Kick off your staycation with A Room for Animal Intelligence. Get your free tickets to visit the museum on April 11 \(or anytime through April 19\) and enjoy enriching activities. This is a whole new way of seeing the animal world curated by Pam McClusky. Come face-to-face with 100 animals from around the globe, rendered at jaw- dropping scale. We're talking the largest wolf, mouse, and mosquito you've ever laid eyes on. The kids won't stop talking about it-and honestly, neither will you. Grab a bite from the Shooby Doo pop-up café \(11 am-3 pm\), listen to live jazz curated by Seattle Jazz Fellowship \(11 am-2 pm\), and join a public collection tour that includes a stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence \(11:30 am\). 10 am-5 pm Build a Beast Brotman Forum Build your very own beast using hand-carved animal stamps created by artist Brian Lane. Pen Pal with an Animal A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Choose your favorite animal in the exhibition to be pen pals with and write to them using the stationary provided in the gallery. Who knows! You might just hear back . . . Wolf Haven International Booth Brotman Forum Visit the Wolf Haven International booth to learn more about wolves and the organization's efforts to restore near-extinct species. Animal-themed Scavenger Hunt A Room for Animal Intelligence Pick up a scavenger hunt created by SAM Docent, David Turner, from the Ticketing Desk and embark on your adventure to discover the wit, wonder, and wisdom of A Room for Animal Intelligence! 10:30 am | Complimentary Pastries for Members Arnold Board Room Start your morning with a delicious pastry made by Shooby Doo Catering \(while supplies last\). 11-11:30 am | Illustrated Storytime with Julie Kim Arnold Board Room Bring your little one to experience the artistic magic of an illustrated animal storytime by artist Julie Kim. 11:30 am-noon | Selfies with Sammy the Camel A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Step into the gallery after storytime to take a selfie with Sammy the Camel! 1-1:30 pm | Wolves 101 Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves from a globally recognized wolf sanctuary, Wolf Haven International, at this engaging talk for kids! 3-4 pm | Wolves in the Pacific Northwest Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves of the Pacific Northwest and what it takes to restore near-extinct populations from Wolf Haven International. Sun Apr 12-Sun Apr 19 Can't make the kick-off event on April 11? Visit any open day during spring break week to write to an animal, build a beast and go on a scavenger hunt. Plus, curator Pam McClusky will be leading a few short tours of the exhibition \(3 pm on Apr 15, 16 and 17\) and all public tours that week will stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence. These activities are free to members and 40% off admission for your guests! Simply reserve museum tickets for April 11. Or visit between the 12 and 19 to celebrate A Room for Animal Intelligence with us.

item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Confluence Park, 595 Rainier Blvd. N, Issaquah, WA

Join Leave No Trace and Green Issaquah for a family-friendly fun volunteer day. Volunteers will work on projects including mulching, removing trash, and removing invasive plants from Confluence Park. Registration is required: bit.ly/leavenotrace\_restoration Leave No Trace is non-profit organization dedicated to protecting the outdoors \(parks, trails, and public lands\) through science-based education that encourages responsible recreation. Along with 11 other cities, Leave No Trace has selected Issaquah as one of its 2026 Spotlights Cities. This program in partnership with Green Issaquah will bring three days of education, outreach, and restoration to parks and open spaces in the city!

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: The 37th Annual Best of the Northwest Spring Show
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The 37th Annual Best of the Northwest Spring Show returns to Hangar 30 at Magnuson Park on April 11–12, 2026, featuring over 100 Pacific Northwest artists selling one-of-a-kind paintings, sculpture, ceramics, jewelry, glass, fiber, and more. Enjoy live artist demonstrations, a special exhibit and youth soapstone carving sessions with the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association, local food vendors, and a community art project — all under one roof. Advance tickets are just $5 \($8 at the door\), kids 12 & under are free, and entry covers both days. Free parking and ADA accessible.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Youth Art Class: Introduction to Embroidery
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Learn the basics of hand embroidery in this beginner-friendly youth workshop inspired by classic Nordic floral motifs. Students will practice simple stitches while creating designs based on leaves, flowers, and natural patterns found in Nordic landscapes. No experience necessary; all materials are provided. This hands-on program offers a calm, creative space to slow down, try something new, and leave with a finished piece and skills to continue stitching at home.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Homebuyer Education Workshop
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: ULMS, 105 14th Avenue, Suite 200, Seattle, WA

This is a free five-hour workshop to help you meet the WA State Housing Finance Commission’s education requirements for down payment assistance and a variety of affordable housing programs and opportunities for qualified applicants! These requirements include the successful completion of a Homebuyers Education Seminar meeting the current FHA, Fannie Mae, FreddieMac, and SMBA benchmark standards.  At the conclusion of the workshop, participants will receive a certificate of achievement from the WA State Housing Finance Commission and DFI valid for two years from the date of issuance.  To join our next workshop, please complete and submit the following registration form at least 24-hours before your selected workshop date. Once this form has been submitted, be sure to check your email for a digital confirmation, as well as additional follow up and important information from a ULMS Housing Department staff member.   In-person workshops are held every 1st and 2nd Saturday of the month from 10:00AM – 3:30PM. They will be held at our Main Office at the following address: 105 14th Ave, Suite 200 Seattle, WA 98122.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Foraging for Basketry and Wild Crafts
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Taught by basket maker and teacher Erin Cox. Join the conversation about the incredible plants that grow here in the Pacific Northwest and explore ways to identify, gather, store and use them to create baskets and crafts that bring nature inside. Whether you live in an urban, suburban or rural setting, there are forage-able crafting and weaving materials to be found. Daylily, daffodil, dandelion, crocosmia, iris, cattails, juncus, bulrush, ivy, honeysuckle, wisteria, dogwood, willow and more! Erin will share about her approach to sustainable and responsible foraging. We should all care for the plants we harvest and preserve the environments they grow in. This discussion is good for beginners and you will walk away with some fun, easy ways to get started. $17.50 BBGS Member/$25 Non-Member Erin found her life’s passion in basketry and wild crafting. In 2024 she established a small basketry willow farm in Carnation, Washington, where she invites students to learn, harvest, and engage with the materials. In 2025, she opened Field and Forest Crafts and her own private teaching studio in NE Seattle.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Family Saturday | Get Ready for Baby Rave
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Olympic Sculpture Park, 2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA

Gear up for Baby Rave by transforming everyday items into vibrant wearable works of art with guidance from teaching artist, Claudia Villalobos! Upcycle materials including cardboard, fabric scraps, and repurposed embellishments to design wings, crowns, skirts and other wearable pieces that sparkle, reflect light, and show off your family's creativity. Event details

item.title: Foraging for Basketry and Wild Crafts
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Taught by basket maker and teacher Erin Cox. Join the conversation about the incredible plants that grow here in the Pacific Northwest and explore ways to identify, gather, store and use them to create baskets and crafts that bring nature inside. Whether you live in an urban, suburban or rural setting, there are forage-able crafting and weaving materials to be found. Daylily, daffodil, dandelion, crocosmia, iris, cattails, juncus, bulrush, ivy, honeysuckle, wisteria, dogwood, willow and more! Erin will share about her approach to sustainable and responsible foraging. We should all care for the plants we harvest and preserve the environments they grow in. This discussion is good for beginners and you will walk away with some fun, easy ways to get started. $17.50 BBGS Member/$25 Non-Member Erin found her life’s passion in basketry and wild crafting. In 2024 she established a small basketry willow farm in Carnation, Washington, where she invites students to learn, harvest, and engage with the materials. In 2025, she opened Field and Forest Crafts and her own private teaching studio in NE Seattle.

item.title: Member Family Day: Animal Intelligence
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

SAM members, don't have a room with a view booked this spring break? Kick off your staycation with A Room for Animal Intelligence. Get your free tickets to visit the museum on April 11 \(or anytime through April 19\) and enjoy enriching activities. This is a whole new way of seeing the animal world curated by Pam McClusky. Come face-to-face with 100 animals from around the globe, rendered at jaw- dropping scale. We're talking the largest wolf, mouse, and mosquito you've ever laid eyes on. The kids won't stop talking about it-and honestly, neither will you. Grab a bite from the Shooby Doo pop-up café \(11 am-3 pm\), listen to live jazz curated by Seattle Jazz Fellowship \(11 am-2 pm\), and join a public collection tour that includes a stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence \(11:30 am\). 10 am-5 pm Build a Beast Brotman Forum Build your very own beast using hand-carved animal stamps created by artist Brian Lane. Pen Pal with an Animal A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Choose your favorite animal in the exhibition to be pen pals with and write to them using the stationary provided in the gallery. Who knows! You might just hear back . . . Wolf Haven International Booth Brotman Forum Visit the Wolf Haven International booth to learn more about wolves and the organization's efforts to restore near-extinct species. Animal-themed Scavenger Hunt A Room for Animal Intelligence Pick up a scavenger hunt created by SAM Docent, David Turner, from the Ticketing Desk and embark on your adventure to discover the wit, wonder, and wisdom of A Room for Animal Intelligence! 10:30 am | Complimentary Pastries for Members Arnold Board Room Start your morning with a delicious pastry made by Shooby Doo Catering \(while supplies last\). 11-11:30 am | Illustrated Storytime with Julie Kim Arnold Board Room Bring your little one to experience the artistic magic of an illustrated animal storytime by artist Julie Kim. 11:30 am-noon | Selfies with Sammy the Camel A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Step into the gallery after storytime to take a selfie with Sammy the Camel! 1-1:30 pm | Wolves 101 Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves from a globally recognized wolf sanctuary, Wolf Haven International, at this engaging talk for kids! 3-4 pm | Wolves in the Pacific Northwest Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves of the Pacific Northwest and what it takes to restore near-extinct populations from Wolf Haven International. Sun Apr 12-Sun Apr 19 Can't make the kick-off event on April 11? Visit any open day during spring break week to write to an animal, build a beast and go on a scavenger hunt. Plus, curator Pam McClusky will be leading a few short tours of the exhibition \(3 pm on Apr 15, 16 and 17\) and all public tours that week will stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence. These activities are free to members and 40% off admission for your guests! Simply reserve museum tickets for April 11. Or visit between the 12 and 19 to celebrate A Room for Animal Intelligence with us.

item.title: Homebuyer Education Workshop
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: ULMS, 105 14th Avenue, Suite 200, Seattle, WA

This is a free five-hour workshop to help you meet the WA State Housing Finance Commission’s education requirements for down payment assistance and a variety of affordable housing programs and opportunities for qualified applicants! These requirements include the successful completion of a Homebuyers Education Seminar meeting the current FHA, Fannie Mae, FreddieMac, and SMBA benchmark standards.  At the conclusion of the workshop, participants will receive a certificate of achievement from the WA State Housing Finance Commission and DFI valid for two years from the date of issuance.  To join our next workshop, please complete and submit the following registration form at least 24-hours before your selected workshop date. Once this form has been submitted, be sure to check your email for a digital confirmation, as well as additional follow up and important information from a ULMS Housing Department staff member.   In-person workshops are held every 1st and 2nd Saturday of the month from 10:00AM – 3:30PM. They will be held at our Main Office at the following address: 105 14th Ave, Suite 200 Seattle, WA 98122.

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops At Best of the Northwest Art Show
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The Northwest Stone Sculptors Association \(NWSSA\) is excited to offer Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops during the Northwest Art Alliance Best of the Northwest Spring Show on April 11th & 12th at Magnuson Park Hangar 30. Youth can participate an up-close, hands-on experience with creativity by learning to carve their own soapstone animal figurine. Artist instructors from the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association will guide youth participants Ages 7 – 17 through the process of shaping this beautiful, natural material into a figurine to take home. These workshops are designed to promote fine motor skills, 3D spatial reasoning, and artistic confidence. The soapstone and all necessary kid-safe tools and water to minimize dust will be provided. The youth workshops are Free with admission to the Best of the Northwest Show.   Two 90 minute sessions are available, on Saturday April 11th and Sunday, April 12th beginning at 10:30 am and 1:30 pm.  Spots are limited and registration is encouraged.

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - All Events

First Hill (0.9 miles E) Seattle Jazz Vespers features the five members of Deseo Carmin playing “a fiery fusion of Latin rhythms, soulful jazz/funk grooves, and the passionate spirit of flamenco” at Seattle First Baptist, 1111 Harvard Ave. in Seattle. Parking.

From Events12 Seattle

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Coworking Open House Week @ The Cloud Room
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: The Cloud Room, 1424 11th Ave Suite 400, Seattle, WA

Curious about coworking and looking for the perfect spot to get work done and reach your goals? Join us for a complimentary day during our upcoming Open House Week and experience what it’s like to be a part of our incredible community! What's Included: One Free Trial Day: Pick any day during Open House Week to try out our coworking space, free of charge! Arrive anytime 9am or later — whatever works best with your schedule. Access to our beautiful workspace including quiet desk space with monitors, large lounge area, phone booths, bar, and outdoor patio. Complimentary coffee, tea, snacks and kombucha. High-speed Wi-Fi, printing, and meeting rooms available for rent The chance to meet like-minded professionals and get a feel for the Cloud Room vibe. Special Offer: If you love your free day with us and decide to become a member, we’ll waive your initiation fees when you sign up! How to Join: Reserve your free trial day by selecting one day of your choice on our Calendly \(one free trial day per person\) Show up and enjoy your day at Cloud Room! Be sure to check in with the staff at the front of the lounge so we can give you a quick tour of the space and get you set up with the wifi. Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! Current Promotions: ⭐️ Sign up for a grounded membership and get your first month free \+ 10% off you first year of membership. 3 month commitment required. ⭐️ Floating Membership Promo: Take 50% off your first full month of floating membership. Spaces are limited—RSVP today.

item.title: Spy Maters Camp II
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Location: Suncatcher Forest School, 18701 se 60th st, Issaquah, WA

Calling all young secret agents! This time kids are welcome to join action-packed Outdoor Spy Camp, where kids will train to become top-notch spies through thrilling challenges, daring missions, and teamwork! Obstacle Courses & Ninja Training – Agents-in-training will test their agility, speed, and stealth as they navigate exciting ninja courses, crawl through the forest and learn to use nature objects to complete the challenge. Scavenger Hunts & Riddle Solving – Spies must sharpen their detective skills by decoding secret messages, solving riddles, and uncovering hidden clues to complete their top-secret missions. Nerf Battles & Tactical Challenges – Gear up for friendly Nerf wars, where teamwork and strategy are key to outsmarting the opposition. Agents will also practice their precision and accuracy with target practice and stealth maneuvers. Escape Room-Style Puzzles – Secret agents will work together to crack codes, unlock treasure chests, and solve mind-bending puzzles before time runs out! Outdoor Adventure & Camouflage Training – Spies will blend into their surroundings, practice silent movement, and use nature to their advantage while completing their missions. This high-energy camp is perfect for kids who love adventure, problem-solving, and action-packed fun in the great outdoors! Are you ready to accept the mission? - follow the link to book a spot!

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Cooking from Scratch: Skills, Techniques & Recipes
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Learn how to cook like a pro! Build your confidence in the kitchen while mastering essential cooking methods and techniques.  Whether you’re just starting out or already love to cook, this fun, hands-on class will help you master the building blocks of great cooking. Learn how to handle a knife with confidence, prepare proteins, and turn fresh vegetables and hearty starches into flavorful dishes. Explore the secrets behind rich stocks and silky sauces, as well as the magic of hydrocolloids \(for thickening and gelling\).  Discover how to balance flavors with finesse, work efficiently with smart kitchen mechanics, and keep things safe and clean with proper sanitation. Come sharpen your skills, boost your confidence, and enjoy the satisfaction of creating meals that truly shine. Learning Objectives Cooking methods and skills Knife skills  Working with proteins, vegetables and starches Preparing stocks and sauces, and using hydrocolloids Balancing flavors Kitchen mechanics, sanitation and safety  Who Might Enjoy This Class? Home cooks who would like to cook better, diversify what they make, get better results, and be more efficient with their time in the kitchen  Anyone ready to learn cooking fundamentals and improve their skills  Competent cooks and professionals wanting to fill in gaps in their skills and learn from a pro

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

item.title: "Stop the Bleed" class
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Green Lake Community Center, 7201 East Green Lake Dr N, Seattle, WA

Neighborhoods: Green Lake,Greenwood/Phinney Ridge,Ravenna/ Bryant/Roosevelt Contact: Ann Forrest, OEM Volunteer Contact Email: OEM\_StopTheBleed@Seattle.gov Audience: Adults,Senior,Special Nee ds,Teen Pre-Register: Yes Cost: Free Link: https://www.seattle.gov/emergency-management/community-engagement/stop-the-bleed Life-threatening bleeding injuries can happen anytime. item.title: Geeks Who Drink Trivia at Stone's Throw Coffee & Market
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Stone's Throw Coffee & Market, 1218 N 45th St, Seattle, WA

Geeks Who Drink Trivia is an authentic, homegrown pub quiz, where useless knowledge means everything. Our general knowledge quizzes cover everything from literature to lit memes, from pop culture to the history of PopTarts—crafted in creative, multimedia rounds that’ll keep you thinking and laughing all night. Bring your cleverest friends \(or your dumbest, if you want to look extra smart\), or play solo and meet some fresh faces. Free & fun to play, all you need is your phone! Join us at Stone's Throw Coffee & Market every Monday, where the fun starts at 7pm. To learn more, visit geekswhodrink.com.

Event details

item.title: Man of La Mancha
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Theatre Off Jackson, 409 7th Ave S, Seattle, WA

Reboot Theatre Company, in association with Theatre Off Jackson, present an all women/TGNC production of MAN OF LA MANCHA. Directed by Jeannette LeGault, music direction by Aimee Hong , and intimacy and fight choreography by Lee Ann Hittenberger.  Performances run April 3-18, 2026, at Theatre Off Jackson \(409 7th Ave. S. Seattle 98104\) Told through a feminist lens, and performed by an all women/TGNC ensemble, our production of this classic musical explores the ongoing fight for women’s rights, including the pivotal battles of the 21st century. Both urgent and hopeful, this powerful reimagining asks, “What does it mean to dream the impossible dream when your voice, your rights, and your choices are at risk?” Written by Dale Wasserman; Music by Mitch Leigh; Lyrics by Joe Darion; Original production directed by Albert Marre. Man of La Mancha is presented by arrangement with Concord Theatricals on behalf of Tams-Witmark LLC. www.concordtheatricals.com

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Planet Money Live
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Town Hall, 1119 Eighth Avenue (at Seneca Street), Seattle, WA

Join the team behind the popular podcast Planet Money and celebrate the publication of their new book, Planet Money: A Guide to the Economic Forces That Shape Your Life. Book author and longtime contributor Alex Mayyasi, Planet Money host Kenny Malone, and visual host Jack Corbett will bring Planet Money’s signature brand of storytelling to the stage and discuss some highlights from the new book, including unlikely origin stories, revealing truths about human behavior, and insightful suggestions for navigating chaotic times drawn from the latest ideas in economics. Daryl Fairweather, Chief Economist at Redfin, will be the special guest this evening. Come prepared to engage with the silly and the sublime and ask some questions of your favorite Planet Money hosts and reporters. Each book purchased with a ticket comes with an exclusive Planet Money tote bag!

item.title: Gabor Maté
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: McCaw Hall, 321 Mercer Street, Seattle, WA

Embark on a profound journey into the intricate landscapes of the human mind and soul with renowned physician, speaker, and bestselling author, Dr. Gabor Maté. With compassion and unwavering authenticity, Dr. Maté explores the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit, shedding light on the underlying causes of human suffering and resilience. Drawing from his groundbreaking research on addiction, trauma, and the impact of early childhood experiences, he offers a transformative perspective on healing and personal growth. Through compelling anecdotes, scientific research, and practical wisdom, Dr. Maté invites audiences to reconsider their understanding of mental health, addiction, and the nature of human connection. Whether you're a healthcare professional, a mental health advocate, or simply a curious seeker of truth, this event promises to inspire, educate, and ignite a deeper understanding of the human condition.

item.title: Twatties Open Mic of the Absurd!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Saint John's Bar and Eatery, 719 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Welcome to Twatties! Seattle’s home to the zany and insane in Capitol Hill. We are an absurdist open mic variety show bringing old-Seattle anti-normative joy. We offer performers four minutes to try anything in front of a live and engaging audience.  All performance art such as standup, music, poetry, drag, and puppets is welcomed. We showcase local art installations, a cozy atmosphere, and great food and drink specials to make Twatties the perfect weirdo third place. Interested in performing? Showcasing your work? Or just looking for a great place to see art and connect with a community? Stage time sign-ups fill fast, so arrive at 7:00 P.M. when the list goes live for the best chance to perform. Audience members wishing to witness the cozy chaos must note the show begins at 8:00 P.M. and has no intermission.” Saint John's Bar and Eatery 719 E Pike St, Seattle, WA 98122 Mondays, starting April 6th. Sign-ups 7:00pm Show 8:00pm EVENT IS FREE 21\+ Must show valid ID
